cataloging_subject.label: Stanzaic Morte Arthur
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English stanzaic romance conventionally titled Stanzaic Morte Arthur, represented by its sole currently recorded, internally lacunose witness in British Library Harley MS 2252, ff. 86r–133v. The subject is the medieval poem as recoverable from that witness, not Harley 2252 as a composite commonplace book, F. J. Furnivall's 1864 or J. Douglas Bruce's 1903 scholarly editions and apparatus, the whole bound EETS carrier, the later Benson/Foster volume that also contains the distinct Alliterative Morte Arthure, the University of Michigan encoding and linked page images, or the checked-in repository XML.

catalog_summary.title: Stanzaic Morte Arthur
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English poet
catalog_summary.editor: J. Douglas Bruce, revising F. J. Furnivall's text after repeated manuscript collations
catalog_summary.date: Competing estimates: c. 1350 or about the end of the fourteenth century; sole witness copied in the second half of the fifteenth century
catalog_summary.region: England; probably the North Midlands, with an older proposal of the northern edge of the North-West Midlands

assertion.notes: This conventional title distinguishes the poem from the Alliterative Morte Arthure and from Malory's prose Morte Darthur.

titles.supplied_by: F. J. Furnivall and J. Douglas Bruce

titles.value: Lordingis that ar leff and dere, lystenyth and I shall you tell

assertion.notes: Bruce reports that the explicit is repeated in different ink but apparently by the same hand.

scope.description: 1864 edition of the poem from Harley MS 2252
1903 re-edition, based on Furnivall and repeatedly collated against Harley MS 2252

assertion.notes: The Database of Middle English Romance gives c. 1350, and Benson/Foster calls a mid-fourteenth-century date a probable guess.

date_statements.display: About the end of the fourteenth century

assertion.notes: Bruce's older dating argument is based chiefly on language. It is retained separately rather than silently reconciled with the c. 1350 estimate.

date_statements.display: Second half of the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: This dates the copying of the target booklet, not the poem's composition and not the early-sixteenth-century compilation of the whole commonplace book.

places.label: North Midlands, England
notes: A probable modern localization of composition, not the London association of the later composite codex.

places.label: Northern edge of the North-West Midlands, England
notes: Bruce's historical localization of the poet's language; he distinguishes it from the language of the two manuscript scribes.

assertion.notes: Both modern sources qualify the localization; it is not a proven place of composition.
This is Bruce's attributed historical proposal, not a synthesis with the modern North Midlands label.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The Latin and later English material elsewhere in Harley MS 2252 belongs to the host codex, not to this poem.

form_statements.description: A stanzaic narrative poem in predominantly eight-line, four-beat stanzas, usually rhyming abababab, with attested irregular groups and an internal lost-leaf lacuna.

assertion.notes: Bruce newly arranged the printed text in eight-line stanzas, while Furnivall says the manuscript writes the lines continuously. The 482 XML LG elements therefore represent editorial stanza analysis, not manuscript graphic divisions.

subject_statements.label: The fall and death of King Arthur

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English romance in eight-line stanzas recounting the affair of Lancelot and Guinevere, the false accusation and defense of the queen, the exposure of the lovers, war between Lancelot and Arthur, Mordred's usurpation, Arthur's fatal final battle, and the deaths and burials of the principal figures. Modern scholarship describes it as a condensation and adaptation of the French La Mort Artu.

The poem is currently recorded in one substantially complete medieval witness, Harley MS 2252, ff. 86r–133v. A missing leaf between ff. 102 and 103 creates an internal lacuna, so 'unique copy' must not be expanded into a claim of physically complete survival. Current DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 lists one witness; the British Library calls it a unique copy; the Database of Middle English Romance likewise records one unique copy and no fragments.

The U-M/General Corpus XML verbally encodes Bruce's title page and poem on printed pp. 1–121. Two front-matter placeholders, one back-matter placeholder, and page-break scaffolding refer to omitted introduction, notes, glossary, index, and other carrier pages; those linked or placeholder-only regions are not additional content parts of the medieval work.

extent.description: One poem occupies one BODY/DIV1 and 482 direct LG TYPE='stanza' groups. All 3,849 XML L elements are direct children of those LGs; none is nested in a note, front matter, back matter, or another division. Fifteen are non-verbal editorial rows (fourteen suffix-numbered dotted/empty rows plus one unnumbered asterisk gap marker), leaving 3,834 non-placeholder verse entries, exactly the count reported in current DIMEV. One of those 3,834 is Bruce's bracketed, Furnivall-supplied line 1413b, expressly absent from the manuscript, so 3,833 entries represent extant manuscript verse. Furnivall's inherited main numbering jumps from 1181 to 1318, reserving 136 integer positions for the lost-leaf lacuna, and ends at 3969. The unnumbered gap marker makes that jump contribute 135 positions relative to raw XML order; fifteen b/c-suffixed L entries do not advance the main integer sequence, reducing the net difference to 120, so 3969 minus 3849 equals 120. Of the 482 LGs, 479 contain eight direct L children, one seven, one six, and one four; after non-verbal placeholders are excluded, the distribution is 471 groups of eight textual entries, two of seven, eight of six, and one of four. The groups follow Bruce's editorial stanza arrangement rather than manuscript layout. One inherited N='3311' between 3407 and 3415 is a source-encoding typo for printed 3411 and is not silently corrected here.

content_parts.label: Stanzaic Morte Arthur
content_parts.summary: The whole recoverable poem, from the opening appeal to listeners through Arthur's fall, the deaths of Lancelot and Guinevere, and the manuscript explicit, with an internal lost-leaf lacuna after line 1181.

locators.value: 86r–133v, with one leaf missing between 102v and 103r

notes: This is a whole-work cataloging part with an internal physical lacuna, not a claim that the sole witness preserves every original line.
The 482 LG groups are not separately cataloged as intrinsic sections.

related_works.title: La Mort Artu
notes: This is a conceptual work-to-work source relation. No particular surviving French manuscript exemplar is identified, and Bruce's more differentiated source argument is not flattened into a physical-source claim.

related_works.title: Alliterative Morte Arthure
notes: The poems are distinct works co-published in the Benson/Foster volume; the Alliterative Morte Arthure is outside aha2659's content boundary.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source, source/CME_phase_1-2/aha2659.xml at commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: complete file; HEADER, TITLEPAGE, BODY/DIV1 TYPE='poem', all LG/L/PB/MILESTONE/NOTE1 elements
evidence.summary: Exact evidence for the AHA2659 identifier, 2006 U-M uncorrected-OCR/TEI Level 1 statement, Bruce title page, poem text and line attributes, 482 LG groups, manuscript and printed-page milestones, omission placeholders, and the encoded boundary.
notes: The exact file is 208,272 bytes and git blob 58f87120c9212844d7b63c0f52fefb0ad47a269b.

evidence.citation: Target-scoped XML ancestry, stanza-size, placeholder, and line-number audit of the pinned aha2659 source.
evidence.locator: all 3,849 L and 482 LG elements; every explicit N attribute; BODY/FRONT/BACK/NOTE ancestry
evidence.summary: Confirmed that every L is a direct child of an LG under the sole BODY poem division and that no L occurs in notes or omitted matter. Counted 3,849 L, 482 LG, fifteen non-verbal placeholder rows, 3,834 non-placeholder verse entries, one supplied line absent from the manuscript, the 1181-to-1318 numbering discontinuity, fifteen suffix-numbered lines, and the isolated inherited 3311-for-3411 attribute error. Direct-L group sizes are 479×8, 1×7, 1×6, and 1×4; textual-entry sizes after placeholder removal are 471×8, 2×7, 8×6, and 1×4.

evidence.citation: J. Douglas Bruce, ed., Le Morte Arthur, Early English Text Society, Extra Series 88 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1903).
evidence.locator: introduction pp. vii–xxx, especially ix–xii and the dialect/date discussion; poem pp. 1–121 = PDF pages 36–156
evidence.summary: Primary edition evidence for the unique Harley witness at ff. 86r–133v, two scribes, internal missing leaf, eight-line stanza analysis, competing date and dialect arguments, source discussion, Furnivall textual base and retained numbering, Bruce's repeated manuscript collations, supplied and placeholder lines, complete poem boundary, and explicit.

evidence.citation: F. J. Furnivall, ed., Le Morte Arthur (London and Cambridge: Macmillan and Co., 1864).
evidence.locator: title page; preface pp. ix–x and xxiv–xxv; poem pp. 1–162 = PDF pages 62–223
evidence.summary: Primary evidence that Furnivall edited from Harley MS 2252, supplied line 1413b, allowed for one or more missing leaves in the numbering, described stanza irregularities and continuous manuscript layout, expanded contractions and added punctuation, and read the text twice against the manuscript.
notes: The Internet Archive item's external metadata incorrectly identifies Bruce's 1903 edition, but internal title-page, preface, and poem evidence identify the cached object as Furnivall 1864.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, AHA2659.0001.001.
evidence.locator: item information, rights statement, contents, and poem division
evidence.summary: Current official item evidence for the Bruce edition identity, 1903 publication, Arthurian-romance subject, stable identifier, public-domain/No Copyright–United States assertion, and the two omitted-front, poem, and omitted-back division structure.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 2252, record 040-002048083.
evidence.locator: title and scope; ff. 86r–133v content entry; date, collation, physical description, provenance, digitised-content and access fields
evidence.summary: Current official evidence that the stanzaic Morte Arthur is a unique copy at ff. 86r–133v, that the target text was copied in the second half of the fifteenth century, that Harley 2252 is a composite paper manuscript later compiled by John Colyns, and that the relevant quire lacks a leaf. The record also states that legacy digital images are currently unavailable and gives current physical-consultation routes.
notes: The record's aggregate 1460–1540 range, English/Latin languages, London ownership, and early-sixteenth-century compilation describe the composite host and are not transferred wholesale to the medieval poem.

evidence.citation: Linne R. Mooney, Daniel W. Mosser, Ian Cornelius, and Michael Johnston, The Digital Index of Middle English Verse: Data Files and Documentation, version 2.0-alpha.1 (2026), record 3252.
evidence.locator: data/Records.xml record-3252 and witness wit-3252-1; data/Manuscripts.xml BLHar2252
evidence.summary: Versioned current repertory evidence for DIMEV 3252 / NIMEV 1994, the conventional title, eight-line abababab stanza form, Arthurian-romance classification, 3,834-line slightly imperfect target, exact ff. 86r–133v locus, incipit, explicit, editions, and one listed Harley witness.
notes: Version 2.0-alpha.1 is a prerelease dated 2026-06-25; the sole-witness claim is scoped to this exact dataset version.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Stanzaic Morte Arthur and London, British Library, MS Harley 2252.
evidence.locator: romance description and manuscript list; Harley contents at ff. 86r–133v
evidence.summary: Current romance-repertory evidence for the preferred title, c. 1350 and North Midlands proposals, eight-line stanza and rhyme forms, plot and themes, modern editions, and a unique Harley copy at ff. 86r–133v. Its 3969-line figure is the inherited conventional terminal lineation, not a count of raw XML L tags or extant manuscript verse entries.
notes: Its early-sixteenth-century/London manuscript label describes the composite host; the current British Library record more specifically dates the target poem's copying to the second half of the fifteenth century.

evidence.citation: Larry D. Benson, ed., revised by Edward E. Foster, King Arthur's Death: The Middle English Stanzaic Morte Arthur and Alliterative Morte Arthure (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 1994).
evidence.locator: preface pp. ix–xii = PDF pages 2–5; Stanzaic Morte Arthur printed pp. 11–123 = PDF pages 16–128; notes pp. 124–128 = PDF pages 129–133
evidence.summary: Modern supporting evidence for anonymous authorship, Middle English verse, probable mid-fourteenth-century/North Midlands composition, unique late-fifteenth-century Harley witness, regularized editorial treatment, derivation from La Mort Artu, plot and themes, and the exact two-work volume boundary. Benson says his stanzaic edition is based mainly on Bruce and read against an unidentified manuscript microfilm.
notes: The cached 297-page image-only PDF is a scan of the 1994 printed two-work edition, not a manuscript facsimile. The Alliterative Morte Arthure begins at PDF page 134 and is outside aha2659.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's date and dialect region be narrowed without collapsing the c. 1350/North Midlands and late-fourteenth-century/northern North-West Midlands proposals?
next_steps: Review recent linguistic and textual scholarship beyond the general repertory and teaching-edition summaries.
Retain materially different proposals as attributed assertions rather than selecting an unsupported compromise.

open_questions.question: How many original lines were lost with the missing Harley leaf?
next_steps: Keep Furnivall's 136 reserved integer positions separate from Bruce's physical estimate of 84 or 92 lost lines.
Consult later critical editions and codicological work before preferring a reconstruction.
notes: Bruce rejects H. O. Sommer's proposed transposition and treats the loss as one leaf; no lost wording is supplied in this manifest.

open_questions.question: Will the British Library restore a complete public digital facsimile of Harley MS 2252?
next_steps: Monitor the official Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue and legacy ARK for restored digital content.
Use the British Library physical-item request, reference enquiry, or full-manuscript reproduction-order routes in the lineage manifest.

notes: The edition chain is not flattened: Bruce explicitly based his text on Furnivall while repeatedly collating Harley; Furnivall describes his text as an accurate rendering read twice against Harley.
The modern Benson/Foster edition is supporting evidence, not the source of the U-M XML, and its cached PDF is a printed-edition scan rather than manuscript photography.

Rights for the medieval work, Furnivall and Bruce editions, U-M encoding, repository XML, Internet Archive/Google derivatives, DIMEV data, METS edition, and any British Library reproduction are recorded separately in the lineage manifest.
